original_abstract:
This study explores the application of Hayden White’s narrative constructivism to the historiographical works of two prominent Slovak exile historians, Milan Stanislav Ďurica and František Vnuk. As exponents of a discourse rooted in the legacy of the Hlinka’s Slovak People’s Party (*ľudácka* historiography), these authors operate from an explicit ideological position that White’s theoretical framework—specifically his concepts of emplotment, historical metaphor, and the interplay between ideology and narrative—is uniquely suited to analyze.

The author demonstrates how Ďurica and Vnuk utilize diverse narrative modes to represent the same historical events, such as the Slovak National Uprising (SNP). While Ďurica employs a tragic plot structure to frame the uprising as a national disaster, Vnuk often shifts toward a satirical or farcical mode to characterize the movement as chaotic and opportunistic. Conversely, both authors utilize a romantic mode to portray the establishment and early years of the first Slovak Republic (1939–1945), framing it as an idyllic or heroic epoch.

The analysis further examines White’s concepts of "refamiliarization" and "detraumatization" as tools for ideological revisionism, particularly regarding the Holocaust and the loss of Slovak statehood. By treating historical texts as literary artifacts, the study highlights how these historians use figurative language and moral allegories to transform facts into a coherent ideologically-charged story. Ultimately, the paper argues that applying White's metahistorical lens reveals how historical "truth" in this context is constructed through aesthetic and pre-figurative choices designed to satisfy the socio-political expectations of a specific target audience.
humanized_abstract:
This paper applies Hayden White’s narrative constructivism to the work of two Slovak exile historians, who are representative of *ľudácka* historiography (the historical discourse of Hlinka’s Slovak People’s Party). The reason for this application is their clear ideological stance, which is reflected in their work and can be interpreted through White’s theories of emplotment, historical metaphor and relationship between ideology and narrative.

The author shows how Ďurica and Vnuk by their use of various narrative modes reflect the different ways in which historical events like the Slovak National Uprising (SNP) are perceived. Ďurica presents the uprising as a national tragedy in the form of tragedy, and Vnuk, on the other hand, usually describes the uprising as absurd and opportunistic in a satirical or farcical manner. Both authors reflect the romantic perception of the establishment and the beginnings of the first Slovak Republic (1939–1945) as a Golden or heroic Age.

This paper builds on the study of White’s concepts of refamiliarization and detraumatization as instrumental for achieving ideological revisionism concerning the Holocaust and the lost Slovak state. White’s theory of metahistory will be applied in order to show how the Slovak historians view historical texts not only as a communication of knowledge and facts but as literary artefacts that express the “moral style” of the group and using rhetorical devices, figures of speech and moral allegories that are intended to mould the historical data into an ideologically significant narrative. Finally, the paper will assert that, based on the application of metahistorical principles on the representation of historical truth in the discourse of the aforementioned historians, it may be possible to determine which aesthetic and pre-figurative choices were used to produce an image of reality which could satisfy the social and political demands of the intended audience.
